IG(Y|X) is often described directionally because it is used for predicting a chosen target Y, but its standard definition H(Y)−H(Y|X) equals mutual information. Mutual information’s symmetry comes from equivalent identities: I(X;Y)=H(Y)−H(Y|X)=H(X)−H(X|Y)=H(X)+H(Y)−H(X,Y). So the same computed value can be interpreted as reduction in uncertainty of Y given X (decision-tree framing) or shared information between X and Y (information-theory framing).

questions.question_text:
Using the contingency table below, compute the information gain IG(Y|X) in bits, where Y = whether someone exercises and X = gym membership.

| | Member | Non-member | Total |
|--|--------|------------|-------|
| Exercises | 40 | 10 | 50 |
| Doesn't exercise | 10 | 40 | 50 |
| Total | 50 | 50 | 100 |

Show the steps to compute H(Y), H(Y|X), and IG(Y|X).
questions.correct_answer: H(Y)=1 bit because P(Y=exercise)=0.5 and P(Y=no)=0.5. For X=member, P(exercise|member)=40/50=0.8 and P(no|member)=0.2 so H(Y|member)=-(0.8 log2 0.8 + 0.2 log2 0.2)≈0.72 bits. For X=non-member, P(exercise|non)=10/50=0.2 and P(no|non)=0.8 so H(Y|non)≈0.72 bits. Since P(X=member)=P(X=non)=0.5, H(Y|X)=0.5·0.72+0.5·0.72=0.72 bits. Therefore IG(Y|X)=H(Y)-H(Y|X)=1-0.72=0.28 bits.
questions.explanation: The marginal label distribution is perfectly balanced, so H(Y)=1 bit. Conditioning on X makes Y less uncertain because each group is skewed (0.8/0.2 or 0.2/0.8), giving conditional entropy ≈0.72 bits in each group. Averaging over X yields H(Y|X)≈0.72, so the reduction in uncertainty (information gain) is 0.28 bits. This equals I(X;Y) by the identity I(X;Y)=H(Y)−H(Y|X).

questions.question_text: Which expression correctly states the equivalence between mutual information and information gain when predicting a target variable Y from a feature X?
options: A. I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H(Y|X) = IG(Y|X)
B. I(X;Y) = H(Y|X) - H(Y) = IG(Y|X)
C. I(X;Y) = H(X,Y) - H(X) - H(Y) = IG(Y|X)
D. I(X;Y) = H(X|Y) - H(Y|X) = IG(Y|X)
questions.explanation: Mutual information can be written as I(X;Y) = H(Y) − H(Y|X). In decision-tree/feature-selection language, this same quantity is called the information gain of Y given X: IG(Y|X) = H(Y) − H(Y|X). The other options either flip the sign (B), use an incorrect identity (C), or mix conditional entropies in a way that is not equal to mutual information (D).

questions.question_text:
You plan to estimate a population mean from a very large dataset (finite population) by sampling without replacement. You want the i.i.d.-with-replacement standard error \(\sigma/\sqrt{n}\) to be accurate to within 1% in **standard deviation** (i.e., you want the FPC multiplier to be at least 0.99):

\[
\sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}} \ge 0.99.
\]

Derive an approximate condition on the sampling fraction \(f=n/N\) (assume \(N\) is large so \(N-1\approx N\)). Then interpret what this implies about when it is reasonable to treat the draws as “effectively i.i.d.”
questions.correct_answer: Using \(N-1\approx N\), the condition becomes \(\sqrt{(N-n)/N}=\sqrt{1-f}\ge 0.99\). Squaring: \(1-f\ge 0.9801\) so \(f\le 0.0199\). Thus the sampling fraction should be about 2% or less. Interpretation: if you sample at most ~2% of a very large finite population, the dependence from sampling without replacement reduces the SD by under 1%, so treating observations as approximately i.i.d. (and using \(\sigma/\sqrt{n}\)) is typically reasonable.
questions.explanation: The FPC affects the SD multiplicatively by \(\sqrt{(N-n)/(N-1)}\). For large \(N\), this is approximately \(\sqrt{1-n/N}=\sqrt{1-f}\). Requiring this to be within 1% of 1 leads to a bound on \(f\). This formalizes the heuristic “if \(n\ll N\), dependence is negligible.”

questions.question_text:
A finite population has size \(N=10{,}000\) with population mean \(\mu\) and population variance \(\sigma^2\). You take a simple random sample without replacement of size \(n=400\) and compute \(\bar Y\).

1) Using the finite-population CLT approximation, write the approximate distribution of \(\bar Y\).
2) Compute the standard error of \(\bar Y\) in terms of \(\sigma\), including the finite-population correction (FPC).
questions.explanation: The finite-population CLT for sampling without replacement gives a Normal approximation with variance \(\sigma^2/n\) multiplied by the FPC \((N-n)/(N-1)\), reflecting reduced variability from negative dependence. The standard error is the square root of that variance. Substituting the given \(N\) and \(n\) yields an FPC of \(\sqrt{9600/9999}\approx 0.9799\), slightly smaller than 1.

questions.question_text: When sampling a simple random sample of size n **without replacement** from a finite population of size N, which statement is most accurate about the sampled values \(Y_1,\dots,Y_n\)?
options: They are i.i.d. because each unit has the same probability of being selected at each draw.
They are independent but not identically distributed, because later draws have different selection probabilities.
They are exchangeable but not strictly independent, because selecting one unit changes the probabilities for the remaining units.
They are neither exchangeable nor independent, because the order of selection changes their joint distribution.
questions.explanation: In sampling without replacement, the draws are not independent: once an element is selected, it cannot appear again, inducing negative dependence and slightly changing conditional probabilities. However, under simple random sampling, the joint distribution is symmetric with respect to permutations of the draw order, so the sequence is exchangeable (the order doesn’t matter).

questions.question_text:
A team wants a cbq plot to meet the "almost textbook-smooth" criterion \(\text{smoothness} > 0.9\). Using the definition
\[
\text{smoothness}=\frac{1}{1+\operatorname{std}(\Delta)},
\]
(a) derive the maximum allowable \(\operatorname{std}(\Delta)\) to satisfy the criterion, and (b) decide whether a plot with \(\operatorname{std}(\Delta)=0.12\) qualifies.
questions.correct_answer: (a) Require 1/(1+std) > 0.9. Solve: 1 > 0.9(1+std) = 0.9 + 0.9std => 0.1 > 0.9std => std < 0.1/0.9 = 1/9 ≈ 0.111... . (b) std = 0.12 is greater than ≈0.111..., so it does not qualify (its smoothness would be 1/1.12 ≈ 0.893).
questions.explanation: The key is to translate a smoothness threshold into a constraint on the standard deviation of first-differences. Because smoothness decreases monotonically as \(\operatorname{std}(\Delta)\) increases, solving the inequality gives a hard upper bound on \(\operatorname{std}(\Delta)\). Comparing 0.12 to the derived bound shows it falls short of the >0.9 target.

questions.question_text: A cbq plot has \(\operatorname{std}(\Delta)=0.2\), where \(\Delta\) are first-differences across quartiles. Using \(\text{smoothness}=\frac{1}{1+\operatorname{std}(\Delta)}\), which interpretation best matches this plot according to the rule of thumb?
options: Almost textbook-smooth rise across quartiles
Good overall, with some small irregularities
Consider inspecting for bins where the observed rate dips or jumps too much
Perfectly linear slope
questions.explanation: Compute smoothness: \(1/(1+0.2)=1/1.2\approx 0.833\). The rule of thumb says \(0.8 < \text{smoothness} \le 0.9\) corresponds to a "good" slope with some small irregularities. It is not > 0.9 (textbook-smooth) and not \(\le 0.8\) (inspect for problematic jumps/dips).

questions.question_text:
Given the smoothness definition
\[
\text{smoothness} = \frac{1}{1 + \operatorname{std}(\Delta)}, \quad \Delta_i = obs_{i+1}-obs_i,
\]
what smoothness value corresponds to a perfectly linear, equal-step slope (i.e., all first-differences are identical)?
options: It cannot be determined without the number of observations
questions.explanation: If all first-differences are identical, then the variability of \(\Delta\) is zero: \(\operatorname{std}(\Delta)=0\). Plugging into the formula gives \(\text{smoothness}=1/(1+0)=1.0\). The number of observations does not matter for this conclusion.

questions.question_text:
You are training a model with two features measured in different units:

- Feature X: typical values around 0–1, with \(\sigma_X = 0.05\)
- Feature Y: typical values around 0–1000, with \(\sigma_Y = 200\)

Suppose you use a linear model with squared error loss and gradient descent. Consider a single data point where both features are 2 standard deviations above their respective means.

Which statement best explains what happens if you *do not* standardize the features (i.e., you use raw \(x\) and \(y\) values) versus if you do standardize (use z-scores)?
options: A. Without standardization, the gradient contributions from X and Y will be comparable because both are 2σ above their means; standardization makes them incomparable.

B. Without standardization, Y is likely to dominate parameter updates because its raw numeric scale makes its gradient terms larger; standardization tends to balance contributions by making both deviations unitless.
C. Without standardization, the model cannot converge at all; standardization guarantees convergence for any learning rate.
D. Without standardization, the mean of the gradients becomes exactly zero; standardization makes the mean nonzero.
questions.explanation: In many gradient-based methods, update magnitudes depend on raw feature values. Even if both features are equally extreme relative to their own spread (2σ), the raw value of Y can be much larger numerically, producing larger gradient components and dominating learning. Standardizing converts both to comparable unitless scales, so updates reflect relative deviation rather than absolute magnitude. A reverses the effect. C is too strong: standardization helps conditioning but does not guarantee convergence for any learning rate. D is not generally true.

questions.question_text:
Two reward distributions come from different tasks:

- Task A: mean \(\mu_A = 10\), standard deviation \(\sigma_A = 1.4\)
- Task B: mean \(\mu_B = 100\), standard deviation \(\sigma_B = 14.1\)

You observe rewards \(r_A = 13\) and \(r_B = 130\).

1) Compute each z-score.
2) Explain in one or two sentences how dividing by \(\sigma\) prevents magnitude from dominating when comparing these rewards.
questions.correct_answer:
1) z_A = (13-10)/1.4 ≈ 2.14; z_B = (130-100)/14.1 ≈ 2.13 (approximately equal).
2) Although Task B has a much larger raw difference (30 vs 3) due to scale, dividing by σ measures how extreme each reward is relative to its own variability. This makes the two rewards comparably “impressive” (about 2 standard deviations above their means) instead of letting the larger numeric scale dominate.
questions.explanation: The raw deviations (3 vs 30) reflect different units/scales, not necessarily different extremeness. Standardization converts both to a common, unitless scale (standard deviations from the mean), enabling fair comparison across tasks and preventing large-magnitude tasks from appearing artificially better.

questions.question_text:
In z-score standardization, why do we divide by the standard deviation (σ) when computing

\[
 z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}
\]

in contexts where different variables/tasks have different numeric scales?
options: A. To convert values into probabilities between 0 and 1
B. To remove the effect of units/scale so that relative deviation (in standard deviations) is comparable across groups
C. To ensure the mean becomes 1 and the variance becomes 0
D. To make all datasets have the same number of samples
questions.explanation: Dividing by σ rescales deviations from the mean into “number of standard deviations,” making scores unitless and comparable across groups with different magnitudes. This prevents a large-scale variable/task from producing larger raw differences (and thus dominating updates) solely due to its numeric scale. A describes min-max scaling (not z-scores). C is incorrect (z-scoring makes mean 0 and variance 1, not mean 1/variance 0). D is unrelated.

questions.question_text:
Two models are evaluated on the same calibration bins using the same smoothness definition
\[
\text{smoothness} = \frac{1}{1 + \mathrm{std}(\Delta)}.
\]
Model X has \(\mathrm{std}(\Delta)=0.12\). Model Y has \(\mathrm{std}(\Delta)=0.25\).

Which statement is most accurate given the interpretation bands in the source content?
options: A. Model X is in the 0.90–1.00 band (very smooth), while Model Y is in the 0.80–0.90 band (generally smooth).
B. Both models are in the 0.90–1.00 band because both standard deviations are less than 1.
C. Model Y is smoother than Model X because larger \(\mathrm{std}(\Delta)\) indicates more consistent increments.
D. Both models must be below 0.80 because any nonzero \(\mathrm{std}(\Delta)\) indicates noticeable unevenness.
questions.explanation: Compute smoothness: X = 1/(1+0.12) ≈ 0.8929 (in 0.80–0.90, but very close to 0.90); Y = 1/(1+0.25) = 0.8 (at the boundary of 0.80–0.90). Among the choices, A best matches the intended band interpretation: X is smoother than Y and near the “very smooth” region, while Y sits at the lower end of “generally smooth.” B is incorrect because the thresholding is on smoothness, not whether std<1. C reverses the relationship (larger std means less smooth). D is incorrect because small nonzero std can still yield smoothness well above 0.80.

questions.question_text:
Suppose the smoothness score is defined as
\[
\text{smoothness} = \frac{1}{1 + \mathrm{std}(\Delta)}
\]
where \(\Delta_i = \text{obs}_{i+1}-\text{obs}_i\). A model has smoothness score 0.85.

1) Compute \(\mathrm{std}(\Delta)\).
2) Based on the guideline ranges, state whether you would be worried about rank-ordering problems and why.
questions.correct_answer:
1) From s = 1/(1+std), std(Δ) = (1/s) - 1 = (1/0.85) - 1 ≈ 1.17647 - 1 = 0.17647.
2) Not typically worried: 0.85 falls in the 0.80–0.90 band (“generally smooth with minor irregularities”). The content says concern starts when dipping below 0.80, where jumps become large enough to suggest rank-ordering problems.
questions.explanation: Rearrange the formula: s = 1/(1+σ) ⇒ 1/s = 1+σ ⇒ σ = 1/s − 1. The qualitative interpretation follows the provided thresholds: 0.85 indicates mostly smooth behavior with small deviations; the warning threshold is <0.80 per the prompt.

questions.question_text:
A calibration curve has a smoothness score of 0.85, computed as
\[
\text{smoothness} = \frac{1}{1 + \mathrm{std}(\Delta)}, \quad \Delta_i = \text{obs}_{i+1}-\text{obs}_i.
\]
Which interpretation best matches the provided guideline ranges?
options: A. Perfectly even rises; ideal calibration curve
B. Generally smooth with minor irregularities; good but not perfect
C. Noticeable unevenness or dips/jumps; likely rank-ordering problems
D. The curve must be non-monotonic because 0.85 is below 0.90
questions.explanation: The source content states 0.80–0.90 corresponds to “generally smooth with minor irregularities,” and specifically calls 0.85 “good but not perfect.” Option A corresponds to 1.00, and option C corresponds to <0.80. Option D is incorrect because a score below 0.90 does not imply non-monotonicity; it indicates more irregularity, not necessarily reversals.

questions.question_text: Two performances have the same `ma_ratio_last_to_first = 1.0`. Which statement is most accurate about what you can conclude from that ratio alone?
options: A. The tempo was constant throughout, so `ma_variance` must be low
B. There was no overall change from start to finish, but there could still be substantial fluctuations in between
C. The moving average trend slope must be exactly zero for any sequence with ratio 1.0
D. The second half must be slower than the first half, so `ma_acceleration` must be positive
questions.explanation: `ma_ratio_last_to_first = 1.0` only indicates the last smoothed value equals the first smoothed value (no net proportional change from start to end). It does not rule out mid-sequence speeding up and slowing down, so variance could be high and the slope/acceleration could be non-zero depending on the path taken. Options A, C, and D incorrectly infer stronger constraints than the ratio provides.

questions.question_text:
A sequence of moving-average interval values (already smoothed) is:

`[100, 105, 110, 115, 120, 130, 140, 150]` (milliseconds)

Using the definition: `ma_acceleration = mean(second_half) - mean(first_half)`, compute `ma_acceleration` and interpret the sign.
questions.correct_answer: First half mean = (100+105+110+115)/4 = 107.5. Second half mean = (120+130+140+150)/4 = 135. ma_acceleration = 135 - 107.5 = 27.5 ms. It is positive, meaning the sequence is slowing down more in the second half (intervals are larger later, indicating increasing deceleration toward the end).
questions.explanation: By definition, `ma_acceleration` compares average moving-average values in the second half versus the first half. A positive difference means later intervals (second half) are larger than earlier ones, so the tempo has shifted toward longer intervals later—interpreted as more slowing down toward the end (second-order trend).

questions.question_text: In moving-average analytics of time intervals between notes, what does a **positive** value of `ma_trend_slope` indicate?
options: A. Intervals are getting shorter over time (speeding up), because the moving average is decreasing
B. Intervals are getting longer over time (slowing down), because the moving average is increasing
C. The timing is consistent, because the moving average has low variance
D. The last interval is larger than the first, because the last moving-average value is the maximum
questions.explanation: `ma_trend_slope` is the slope from a linear regression fit to the moving-average values over time. A positive slope means the moving average tends to increase as time progresses, implying intervals are getting longer on average (deceleration/slowing down). The other options confuse slope with variance, last value, or a max comparison.

questions.question_text:
Mutual information is defined as I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) − H(X,Y) and information gain as IG(Y|X) = H(Y) − H(Y|X).

(a) Prove (using entropy identities) that for discrete variables, IG(Y|X) = I(X;Y).

(b) Explain one practical reason why a high-cardinality feature (e.g., an ID) can appear to have high estimated mutual information with Y in finite data, even if it is not genuinely predictive.

Your answer should be conceptual and may include equations.
questions.correct_answer:
(a) Use the joint-entropy identity H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y|X). Substitute into I(X;Y):
I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) − H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y) − [H(X) + H(Y|X)] = H(Y) − H(Y|X) = IG(Y|X).
Thus, for discrete variables, information gain (reduction in uncertainty about Y from knowing X) equals mutual information (shared information).

(b) With high-cardinality X, many categories have very few samples. Estimating p(y|x) from sparse counts can overfit noise: the empirical conditional entropy Ĥ(Y|X) can be biased downward because some categories accidentally look “pure” in the sample. That makes Î(X;Y) = Ĥ(Y) − Ĥ(Y|X) spuriously large even when X and Y are independent in the population. This is an estimation/overfitting issue (finite-sample bias), often mitigated with regularization, cross-validation, permutation tests, or bias-corrected MI estimators.
questions.explanation:
(a) The key step is recognizing the chain rule for entropy: H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y|X). Plugging this into the MI definition cancels H(X) and leaves exactly H(Y) − H(Y|X), which is the information gain definition.

(b) Mutual information requires estimating probabilities. High-cardinality features create many low-count bins/categories, making probability estimates noisy. This can artificially reduce the estimated conditional entropy and inflate estimated MI, even when the true MI is near zero.

questions.question_text:
A binary target Y indicates whether a user buys a product. You evaluate two candidate features:

- Feature A: a random user ID with many unique values (high cardinality)
- Feature B: a binary indicator of whether the user is a previous customer

Assume A is independent of Y, while B is strongly predictive of Y.

Which outcome is most consistent with entropy/information-gain reasoning?
options: A. A has high H(A) and therefore high IG(Y|A); B has low H(B) and therefore low IG(Y|B).
B. A has high H(A) but IG(Y|A) ≈ 0; B may have lower H(B) but IG(Y|B) is high.
C. A must have IG(Y|A) > 0 because it has many categories; B must have IG(Y|B) = 0 because it is binary.
D. Both A and B will have the same IG because IG depends only on H(Y).
questions.explanation: High entropy in a feature (many unique values) does not imply usefulness for predicting Y. If A is independent of Y, then H(Y|A) = H(Y), so IG(Y|A) = H(Y) − H(Y|A) ≈ 0. A binary feature can still be highly informative: if B reduces uncertainty about Y a lot, then H(Y|B) ≪ H(Y), so IG(Y|B) is high. This distinguishes “variability in X” (entropy) from “predictive power about Y” (information gain).

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes the difference between entropy H(X) and information gain IG(Y|X)?
options: A. Entropy measures uncertainty in a single variable, while information gain measures how much knowing X reduces uncertainty about Y.
B. Entropy measures linear dependence between X and Y, while information gain measures monotonic dependence.
C. Entropy is symmetric in X and Y, while information gain is always symmetric in X and Y.
D. Entropy can only be computed for continuous variables, while information gain is only for categorical variables.
questions.explanation: Entropy H(X) quantifies uncertainty in one random variable X. Information gain IG(Y|X) = H(Y) − H(Y|X) quantifies the reduction in uncertainty about a target Y when you know a feature X. Correlation-related claims in B are incorrect (entropy/IG are not limited to linear/monotonic). C is incorrect because entropy is not a measure between two variables (mutual information is symmetric; IG is directional). D is incorrect because entropy/IG are naturally suited to discrete variables; continuous variables require estimation/discretization.

questions.question_text:
A classifier uses a categorical feature X to help predict a label Y. You are told:

- X is constant in the dataset (it always takes the same value).

Answer the following:
1) What is H(X)?
2) From the “before observation” perspective, what does this imply about uncertainty in X?
3) From the “after observation” perspective, what does this imply about information gained by observing X?
4) Briefly explain the implication for feature usefulness in a predictive model.

Use clear reasoning; formulas are optional.
questions.correct_answer:
1) H(X)=0.
2) There is no uncertainty in X before observation because it is perfectly predictable.
3) Observing X yields zero information gain because the observed value is already known with certainty.
4) As a feature, X is not useful for prediction (it cannot distinguish between cases), so it provides no discriminative signal for the model.
questions.explanation: A constant (deterministic) variable has a degenerate distribution: one outcome has probability 1. Entropy is 0 in this case. That simultaneously means zero unpredictability before observation and zero information gained upon observation. In feature analysis, such a feature cannot help explain or predict variation in Y because it never varies.

questions.question_text:
A binary feature X takes values {0,1}. Consider two features:

- Feature A: P(X=1)=0.5
- Feature B: P(X=1)=0.99

1) Which feature has higher entropy?
2) Using the “potential information” intuition, explain which feature is expected to be more informative to observe and why.

(You do not need to compute entropy numerically; a conceptual comparison is sufficient.)
questions.correct_answer:
1) Feature A has higher entropy.
2) Feature A is expected to be more informative to observe because before observing it you are maximally uncertain (balanced outcomes). Observing it resolves more uncertainty on average. Feature B is highly predictable (almost always 1), so you gain little new information from observing it most of the time.
questions.explanation: Entropy is maximized for a Bernoulli variable when p=0.5 (most uncertain) and approaches 0 as p approaches 0 or 1 (almost deterministic). The “potential information” view says higher uncertainty before observation implies more information is available to be gained by observing the outcome.

questions.question_text:
Which statement best captures the relationship between entropy as uncertainty and entropy as information?

Assume entropy is measured for a random variable X before observing its value, and “information gained” refers to what you learn when you do observe X.
options: A. Entropy measures uncertainty before observation, and a different quantity (not entropy) measures information gained after observation.
B. Entropy measures uncertainty before observation, and the same entropy can be interpreted as the expected information gained from observing the outcome.
C. High entropy means low uncertainty but high information, so the two interpretations contradict each other.
D. Entropy only measures information after observation; before observation it is undefined.
questions.explanation: Entropy is a single quantity with two complementary interpretations: before observing X it quantifies unpredictability (uncertainty), and from the perspective of observation it equals the expected information you gain by learning the outcome. These are not competing definitions; they are two framings of the same measure.

questions.question_text:
You are comparing two categorical features measured on the same dataset.

- Feature X has 1000 unique categories, but 99% of observations fall into a single category; the remaining 1% is evenly split among 999 categories.
- Feature Y has 2 categories with probabilities [0.5, 0.5].

Without computing exact values, determine which feature has higher Shannon entropy (in bits), and justify your answer using the ideas of variety and balance.

Then, give an approximate upper bound for the entropy of Feature X using the fact that for any distribution on k categories, H \le \log_2(k).
questions.correct_answer: Feature Y has higher entropy. Y is perfectly balanced across 2 categories, giving H=1 bit. Feature X has high variety but extremely low balance because 99% mass is concentrated in one category, so its entropy is low (well below 1 bit). An approximate upper bound for X is H_X \le \log_2(1000) \approx 9.97 bits, but the true H_X is far smaller due to the 0.99 concentration.
questions.explanation: Entropy increases with both variety and balance. Feature X’s 1000 categories suggest potential for high entropy, but the 0.99 spike makes the outcome highly predictable, which sharply reduces entropy. Feature Y has fewer categories but maximal balance, yielding 1 bit exactly. The bound H \le \log2(k) provides only a ceiling; it is not tight when the distribution is highly imbalanced, which is precisely the case for Feature X.

questions.question_text:
Compute the Shannon entropy (in bits) for a 4-category feature with probabilities [0.97, 0.01, 0.01, 0.01]. Use

\[H = -\sum_i p_i \log_2(p_i)\]

Round to two decimals.

Also briefly interpret the result in terms of variety and balance.
questions.correct_answer: H \approx 0.24 bits. Interpretation: although there are 4 categories (variety), the distribution is extremely imbalanced (low balance) with 97% in one category, so uncertainty is low and entropy is low—close to a near-constant feature.
questions.explanation: Compute: H = -(0.97 log2 0.97 + 3*(0.01 log2 0.01)). Numerically, log2(0.97)≈-0.0439 so contribution ≈0.97*0.0439≈0.0426. log2(0.01)≈-6.6439 so each 0.01 term contributes ≈0.0664; times 3 gives ≈0.1993. Total ≈0.2419 bits → 0.24 bits. Many categories do not guarantee high entropy; balance matters strongly.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes what Shannon entropy measures for a categorical feature?
options: It increases only with the number of distinct categories, regardless of their frequencies.
It increases when there are more categories and the distribution across them is more even.
It is maximized when one category dominates the distribution.
It depends only on the sample size, not on the distribution of categories.
questions.explanation: Shannon entropy captures both (1) variety (how many categories have nonzero probability) and (2) balance (how evenly probability mass is spread). Adding categories can increase entropy, but if the distribution is highly imbalanced, entropy can still be low.

questions.question_text:
A researcher computes DiD using different observation windows:
- Treatment group change from Jan→Jun (6 months): +600
- Control group change from Feb→May (3 months): +200
They report DiD = 600 − 200 = 400.

Explain why this DiD estimate is not valid under the standard DiD setup, and describe a concrete fix to make the design compatible with DiD (assume you can collect or reselect measurements).
questions.correct_answer: It is invalid because the treatment and control changes are computed over different calendar time points and different durations (6 months vs 3 months). That means the groups are exposed to different time-varying external factors (seasonality, macro shocks, market changes), so the difference in changes cannot be attributed to treatment under the parallel trends logic. A fix is to align the pre and post measurement dates for both groups (e.g., measure both groups at Jan 1 and Jun 1), or restrict/aggregate data so both groups’ changes are computed over the same Jan→Jun window (or the same Feb→May window) with identical time points and duration.
questions.explanation: Standard DiD relies on comparing like-with-like time changes. When time windows differ, the control group is not a valid counterfactual for the treatment group’s time path because it experiences a different segment of the calendar and a different length of exposure to external influences. Aligning time points (same before and after dates for both groups) restores comparability and supports the interpretation that remaining differences in changes are due to treatment rather than mismatched timing.

questions.question_text:
You plan a DiD study of a training program introduced on 2024-03-01. Which data structure is valid for a standard two-period DiD analysis?

Option A:
```python
data = {
  'group': ['treatment','treatment','control','control'],
  'date':  ['2024-01-01','2024-06-01','2024-02-01','2024-05-01'],
  'value': [100,150,95,110]
}
```

Option B:
```python
data = {
  'group': ['treatment','treatment','control','control'],
  'date':  ['2024-01-01','2024-06-01','2024-01-01','2024-06-01'],
  'value': [100,150,95,110]
}
```

Option C:
```python
data = {
  'group': ['treatment','control'],
  'date':  ['2024-06-01','2024-06-01'],
  'value': [150,110]
}
```

Option D:
```python
data = {
  'group': ['treatment','treatment','control','control'],
  'date':  ['2024-01-01','2024-06-01','2024-01-01','2024-04-01'],
  'value': [100,150,95,105]
}
```
options: A
B
C
D
questions.explanation: A valid two-period DiD requires both groups observed in the same pre period and the same post period. Option B measures both treatment and control on 2024-01-01 (before) and 2024-06-01 (after). Options A and D use different time points for the control group, creating different durations/external conditions. Option C lacks a pre-period entirely, so it cannot form within-group changes.

questions.question_text: In a standard Difference-in-Differences (DiD) design, how should the treatment and control groups be measured relative to the “before” and “after” periods?
options: A. They must be measured at the exact same time points (same before date and same after date) for both groups.
B. They can be measured at different time points as long as both have one “before” and one “after” measurement.
C. They must be measured at the same time points only if the outcome is seasonal; otherwise it does not matter.
D. They should be measured at different time points to avoid spillover effects.
questions.explanation: DiD compares changes over time between groups. If groups are not measured at the same time points (and thus over the same duration), they may experience different external conditions (seasonality, macroeconomic shocks, competitor actions). That breaks the logic behind DiD and undermines the parallel trends interpretation.

questions.question_text:
Consider the following calibration approach for group g:

- For each fold k, train a classifier on the fold’s training set.
- On the fold’s validation set, compute
  \(\Delta_{g,k} = \hat{p}_{\text{actual}}(g,k) - \hat{p}_{\text{pred}}(g,k)\)
  where \(\hat{p}_{\text{actual}}\) is the empirical positive rate in group g within that fold’s validation data, and \(\hat{p}_{\text{pred}}\) is the mean predicted probability for those same rows.
- Average across folds: \(\Delta_g = \frac{1}{K} \sum_k \Delta_{g,k}\)
- Calibrate all probabilities: \(p' = \text{clip}(p + \Delta_g, 0, 1)\)

In very small groups, this can create noticeable self-influence and unstable deltas.

Propose an advanced, statistically grounded modification that (i) reduces instability for small groups and (ii) minimizes self-influence when reporting metrics. Your answer should describe the method at a high level and explain why it helps.
questions.correct_answer:
One strong modification is to use a shrinkage / hierarchical (partial pooling) group calibrator fit on out-of-fold predictions, combined with strictly separated evaluation.

(i) Reduce instability for small groups: replace the raw per-group delta with a regularized estimate that shrinks small-group adjustments toward a global adjustment (or toward 0). For example, fit a mixed-effects calibration model on OOF data such as:
- logit(p_true) ≈ logit(p_pred) + b_g, with b_g ~ Normal(0, τ^2)
or a simpler empirical Bayes shrinkage on deltas where the estimated Δ_g is a weighted average of the group’s observed delta and the overall delta, with weights depending on group sample size. This reduces variance because tiny groups no longer get extreme corrections driven by noise.

(ii) Minimize self-influence in reported metrics: use nested CV or a final untouched test set. In nested CV, within each outer fold, fit the calibrator (including shrinkage hyperparameters like τ) and select any threshold using only the outer-training portion (possibly via inner CV), then evaluate on the outer-validation fold. This ensures the fold being scored did not contribute labels to its own calibration/threshold choices.

Why it helps: shrinkage addresses high-variance delta estimates in small groups (bias-variance trade-off), and nesting (or a final holdout) prevents using the same labels both to tune calibration/thresholds and to evaluate, removing optimistic bias from self-influence.
questions.explanation: Raw per-group deltas are noisy when group counts are small; partial pooling (hierarchical modeling or empirical Bayes shrinkage) stabilizes estimates by borrowing strength across groups. Separating calibration/threshold fitting from evaluation via nesting or a final holdout prevents the evaluation fold’s labels from influencing the calibrator used to score that fold, which addresses the subtle self-influence described in cross-validated calibration pipelines.

questions.question_text:
You run K-fold CV and obtain out-of-fold (OOF) predicted probabilities for every row. You then compute per-group deltas using the OOF predictions and true labels, and apply those deltas to the OOF probabilities. Finally, you choose a classification threshold that maximizes F1 on these calibrated OOF probabilities and report that F1 as your model performance.

Explain why this reported F1 can be optimistic, and describe one concrete modification to the evaluation procedure that would reduce or remove this optimism.
questions.correct_answer:
The reported F1 can be optimistic because the calibration deltas (and especially the threshold) are tuned using the same OOF predictions and the same ground-truth labels that are then used to compute the metric. Even though the base model did not train on y_val, the pipeline has effectively used labels to shape outputs and select the threshold, so the evaluation is no longer strictly on untouched data.

A fix is to separate calibration/threshold selection from final evaluation. For example: (1) hold out a final test set; (2) perform CV on the remaining data to generate OOF predictions; (3) fit the group calibrator and choose the threshold using only the training/CV portion; (4) evaluate once on the held-out test set. Alternatively, nest calibration and threshold selection inside each fold and report performance from the fold-wise evaluations (i.e., each fold is evaluated using only calibrator/threshold fit without using that fold’s labels for evaluation).
questions.explanation: Optimism arises when the same labels influence both the tuning step (calibration and/or threshold selection) and the final metric computation. Proper evaluation requires that any step that uses labels to tune parameters be performed on data separate from the data used to report the final metric, via nesting or a final untouched test set.

questions.question_text:
In a cross-validated per-group calibration procedure, you train a classifier on each fold’s training split, then compute a per-group delta on that fold’s validation split as (actual_rate − predicted_rate), average deltas across folds, and add the average delta back to all predicted probabilities.

Which statement best describes whether this constitutes label leakage?
options: A. Yes: validation labels are used to update the classifier’s weights, so the model is trained on validation data.
B. No: validation labels are never used anywhere in the pipeline, so there is zero leakage end-to-end.
C. Partially: the core classifier is not trained on validation labels, but the calibrator is fit using validation labels (a meta-model), which can bias reported metrics if not evaluated properly.
D. Yes: any use of validation labels for any purpose makes the classifier’s training invalid and guarantees severe leakage.
questions.explanation: The classifier training remains clean because each fold’s model.fit uses only (X_train, y_train). However, calibration is itself a fitting step: you use (y_val, proba_val) to estimate deltas, which is effectively fitting a meta-model on labels. This is standard practice when done with out-of-fold predictions, but if you then reuse the same data to choose thresholds or report performance, you can introduce optimistic bias (especially for small groups).

questions.question_text:
Free response:

You are building a per-group calibration adjustment where, for each group g, you compute

- `pred_rate_g = mean(p_i)` over rows in group g
- `actual_rate_g = mean(y_i)` over rows in group g
- `delta_g = actual_rate_g - pred_rate_g`

(a) Explain why computing `delta_g` on training-set probabilities is biased.

(b) Describe two valid workflows to compute `delta_g` without leakage, using (i) post-CV OOF predictions and (ii) nested CV / inner calibration split.

(c) In nested CV, clarify where the deltas are computed and where they are applied (inner vs outer split), and why this preserves out-of-sample integrity.
questions.correct_answer:
(a) Training-set probabilities are in-sample; the model has been optimized on those same rows, so predicted probabilities are typically overly accurate on the training data due to overfitting. As a result, `actual_rate_g - pred_rate_g` computed on training predictions is optimistically biased (it underestimates true miscalibration) and will not generalize to unseen data.

(b) Two leakage-free workflows:
(i) Post-CV (OOF) calibration: Run K-fold CV and for each fold fit the model on `train_idx`, predict probabilities on `val_idx`, and store them into a single `oof_proba[val_idx]`. After all folds, compute `pred_rate_g`, `actual_rate_g`, and `delta_g` using `oof_proba` and `y` across the full dataset. Then train a final model on all training data and apply the learned `delta_g` adjustments to test/new predictions.
(ii) Nested CV / inner calibration split: For each outer fold, further split the outer training portion into an inner-train and inner-calibration (hold-out). Fit on inner-train, predict on inner-calibration to obtain out-of-sample probabilities for the calibration rows, compute `delta_g` on that inner-calibration set, and then apply those deltas to adjust probabilities on the outer validation set. This keeps calibration estimation out-of-sample relative to the model used to generate those probabilities.

(c) In nested CV, deltas are computed on the inner calibration hold-out (rows not used to fit the inner model). Those deltas are then applied to the outer validation predictions (also generated by a model that did not train on the outer validation rows). This preserves out-of-sample integrity because at no point are deltas computed from probabilities on rows that were used to train the model that produced those probabilities; thus both the probability estimates and the delta estimates reflect performance on unseen data within the CV procedure.
questions.explanation: The core concept is avoiding leakage in the calibration step. Calibration deltas must be learned from predictions that are out-of-sample with respect to the model producing them; otherwise, the estimated miscalibration is systematically too small. Post-CV OOF calibration achieves this by assigning each row exactly one prediction from a model trained without that row. Nested CV achieves it by creating an additional hold-out inside each outer fold so that deltas are estimated on an inner set that is not used for fitting, then evaluated/applied on the outer validation set, maintaining separation between fitting, calibration estimation, and evaluation/application.

questions.question_text:
You want to compute mean per-group probability deltas using out-of-fold (OOF) predictions from 5-fold CV. Complete the approach conceptually: which pseudocode best ensures each row’s probability is produced by a model that did not train on that row?

Assume `X`, `y` are full training data, `groups` defines group membership, and `kf` yields `(train_idx, val_idx)`.
options: A. Fit once on all `X, y`, predict on all `X`, then compute deltas by group.
B. For each fold: fit on `train_idx`, predict on `val_idx`, store into a preallocated `oof_proba[val_idx]`; after CV, compute group deltas using `oof_proba` vs `y`.
C. For each fold: fit on `val_idx`, predict on `train_idx`, store into `oof_proba[train_idx]`; after CV, compute group deltas.
D. For each fold: fit on `train_idx`, predict on `train_idx`, average those predictions across folds for each row, then compute deltas.
questions.explanation: OOF predictions require that each row’s prediction be generated by a model that never saw that row during fitting. Option B does exactly that: each fold trains on `train_idx` and predicts on `val_idx`, then writes those probabilities back to the correct rows. After the loop, every row has exactly one out-of-sample probability, enabling unbiased estimation of per-group miscalibration (`actual_rate - pred_rate`) on the OOF set. The other options either use in-sample predictions (A, D) or invert the split (C) which still makes predictions for rows that were used in training for that fold’s model.

questions.question_text:
You compute per-group calibration deltas as `delta = actual_rate - pred_rate` using probabilities from:

```python
proba_train = model.predict_proba(X_train)[:, 1]
```

What is the main statistical issue with learning deltas from `proba_train` and then applying them to new/unseen data?
options: A. It causes optimistic bias because the deltas are computed from in-sample predictions (data leakage into calibration).
B. It causes pessimistic bias because training predictions are noisier than validation predictions.
C. It is unbiased as long as groups are balanced across classes.
D. It is only a problem for linear models; tree-based models are unaffected.
questions.explanation: Using `predict_proba(X_train)` produces in-sample probabilities. Most models fit some noise/structure specific to the training set, so their training predictions are typically closer to the training labels than they will be on unseen data. Computing `actual_rate - pred_rate` on these in-sample probabilities underestimates true miscalibration (optimistically biased). This is a form of leakage: the calibration step is using information from the same data the model was trained on, so it does not reflect out-of-sample performance.

questions.question_text:
A manager wants to compare \(\sum p_i\) (sum of predicted probabilities) to \(\sum y_i\) (sum of actual 0/1 outcomes) by `degree_offering` × `year_quarter` to assess whether the model is over/underpredicting by segment.

(a) Explain why this comparison is statistically meaningful when probabilities are calibrated.

(b) Give one concrete scenario where this comparison could be misleading, and explain why.

(c) Propose one additional check/metric (beyond raw error) you would compute at the group level to better interpret results, and briefly justify it.
questions.correct_answer:
(a) Because if the predicted probabilities are meaningful estimates of \(P(y=1\mid x)\), then by linearity of expectation \(E[\sum y_i] = \sum E[y_i] = \sum p_i\). Thus \(\sum p_i\) is the expected number of positives in the group, and \(\sum y_i\) is the observed number; comparing them diagnoses aggregate calibration/segment bias.

(b) It can be misleading when probabilities are poorly calibrated (e.g., a model systematically outputs probabilities that are too high/low). Another misleading scenario is severe selection/label bias within a group (e.g., outcomes are missing not at random), where \(\sum y_i\) does not reflect the true submit rate. In either case, the gap between \(\sum p_i\) and \(\sum y_i\) may reflect data issues or miscalibration rather than genuine segment performance.

(c) Example additional checks: compute a normalized error such as percent difference \(100\times(\sum p_i-\sum y_i)/\sum y_i\) (or /n), and/or compute a calibration curve / reliability diagram per group, or group-level Brier score. These help compare groups of different sizes and assess calibration quality beyond a single raw count difference.
questions.explanation: A strong answer connects sums of probabilities to expected counts via linearity of expectation, states the calibration assumption explicitly, identifies a realistic failure mode (miscalibration, biased labels, missing outcomes, distribution shift, tiny group sizes leading to high variance), and proposes an additional diagnostic that improves interpretability (normalization, uncertainty/variance, Brier score, calibration plots).

questions.question_text:
You aggregate model outputs by group and compute:

```python
error = sum(predicted_probs) - sum(actuals)
```

For the group `MBA, 2024-Q1`, you have:
- predicted probabilities: [0.6, 0.8, 0.3]
- actual submits (0/1): [1, 1, 0]

Which statement is correct?
options: error = +0.3, so the model underpredicts submits in this group
error = -0.3, so the model underpredicts submits in this group
error = -0.3, so the model overpredicts submits in this group
error = +0.3, so the model is perfectly calibrated in this group
questions.explanation: Sum of predicted probabilities = 0.6 + 0.8 + 0.3 = 1.7. Sum of actuals = 1 + 1 + 0 = 2. Therefore error = 1.7 − 2 = −0.3. A negative error means predicted expected submits are lower than observed submits, i.e., the model underpredicts in aggregate for this group.

questions.question_text: In a dataset where each row has a predicted probability \(p_i = P(\text{submit}=1\mid x_i)\) and an actual binary outcome \(y_i\in\{0,1\}\), what does \(\sum_i p_i\) represent for a given group (e.g., a specific `degree_offering` × `year_quarter`)?
options: The probability that at least one person in the group submits
The expected number of submits in the group
The observed number of submits in the group
The model’s accuracy within the group
questions.explanation: Each \(p_i\) is the model’s predicted probability of a submit for row \(i\). Summing probabilities over a group gives \(\sum_i p_i\), which is the expected count of submits in that group (linearity of expectation). The observed count is instead \(\sum_i y_i\).

questions.question_text:
You bin observations into quartiles by predicted probability and compute, for each quartile i, the mean predicted probability \(\bar p_i\) and observed win-rate \(\bar y_i\).

(a) Define two quantitative diagnostics you would compute from these quartile summaries to assess (i) discrimination and (ii) calibration.

(b) Suppose the observed win-rates are not monotonic (e.g., Q4 observed < Q3 observed). Give two plausible statistical reasons this can happen even if the model is not "completely broken," and propose one practical remedy for each reason.

Your answer should be in terms of the quartile plot concepts (observed slope and predicted-vs-observed gaps).
questions.correct_answer:
(a) Discrimination diagnostic: quantify the monotonic increase of observed win-rate across bins, e.g., check \(\bar y_1 \le \bar y_2 \le \bar y_3 \le \bar y_4\) or compute a slope/correlation between bin index (1..4) and \(\bar y_i\), or compute the difference \(\bar y_4-\bar y_1\) (lift) and/or count monotonicity violations. Calibration diagnostic: compute bin-wise calibration gaps \(g_i = \bar p_i - \bar y_i\) and summarize with max |gap|, mean absolute gap, or an ECE-like weighted average \(\sum_i w_i |g_i|\) where \(w_i\) is the bin fraction.

(b) Two plausible reasons and remedies:
1) Sampling variability / small effective sample size in a bin: Even with correct ranking in expectation, finite samples (especially if wins are rare) can make \(\bar y_i\) noisy so Q4 may dip below Q3 by chance. Remedy: use more data, use fewer/more stable bins (e.g., deciles only if enough data), add confidence intervals/bootstrapping, or apply isotonic regression on the reliability curve.
2) Distribution shift or heterogeneity within bins: The top-score bin may contain a different subpopulation where the conditional win-rate differs (e.g., the model over-scores a subgroup), causing Q4 observed to be lower than Q3. Remedy: stratify the plot by key segments, add interaction features, recalibrate per segment, or retrain with representative data.
(Other acceptable reason/remedy pairs: label noise or leakage causing misranking; remedy: improve labels/feature set and validate properly; systematic miscalibration at extremes; remedy: apply calibration methods like Platt scaling or isotonic regression and re-check gaps.)
questions.explanation: A strong answer distinguishes discrimination (whether higher predicted scores correspond to higher observed rates—captured by the observed slope/monotonicity) from calibration (whether predicted probabilities match observed frequencies—captured by per-bin gaps \(\bar p_i-\bar y_i\)). Non-monotonic observed rates can occur due to noise (variance in \(\bar y_i\)) or real structure (shift/heterogeneity) and should prompt remedies that either reduce variance (more data, fewer bins, uncertainty quantification) or address bias/shift (segmentation, retraining, recalibration).

questions.question_text:
A model's predictions are binned into quartiles by predicted probability. The table shows mean predicted probability and observed win-rate per quartile:

| Quartile | Mean predicted p | Observed win-rate |
|---------:|------------------:|------------------:|
| Q1       | 0.10              | 0.18              |
| Q2       | 0.30              | 0.22              |
| Q3       | 0.55              | 0.40              |
| Q4       | 0.80              | 0.35              |

Which interpretation is most accurate?
options: The model shows good discrimination because predicted probabilities rise by quartile, and it is well-calibrated because gaps are small.
The model shows poor discrimination because observed win-rate is not monotonic, and it is miscalibrated because predicted minus observed gaps are large and vary by quartile.
The model shows good discrimination because observed win-rate is higher in Q4 than Q1, and it is well-calibrated because Q2 is close to Q1.
The model shows perfect discrimination because Q4 has the highest mean predicted probability, and calibration cannot be assessed from quartiles.
questions.explanation: Discrimination (ranking quality) is assessed by whether observed win-rates increase from Q1→Q4. Here, observed rates go 0.18, 0.22, 0.40, then drop to 0.35 in Q4, so the model is not ranking perfectly (non-monotonic observed slope). Calibration per bin can be checked via (predicted − observed): Q1 = 0.10−0.18 = −0.08 (underpredict), Q2 = 0.30−0.22 = 0.08 (overpredict), Q3 = 0.55−0.40 = 0.15 (overpredict), Q4 = 0.80−0.35 = 0.45 (strong overpredict). The gaps are not small and show systematic issues, especially in Q4.

questions.question_text:
You create quartiles by binning customers using their predicted win probability (e.g., qcut on predicted probability). Which statement is TRUE about what you should expect to see in a quartile plot?

(Assume Quartile 1 = lowest predicted probabilities, Quartile 4 = highest.)
options: The mean predicted win-rate will always increase from Quartile 1 to Quartile 4, regardless of model quality.
The actual (observed) win-rate will always increase from Quartile 1 to Quartile 4, regardless of model quality.
If the mean predicted win-rate increases across quartiles, the model must be well-calibrated.
If the mean predicted win-rate increases across quartiles, the model must have strong discrimination.
questions.explanation: When you bin by predicted probability, the bins are defined by ordering predicted scores, so the average predicted probability in higher bins is mechanically larger. This does not guarantee that actual win-rates increase (discrimination) or that predicted and actual match (calibration).

questions.question_text:
You want to build a calibration-by-quartiles table comparing mean predicted probability vs. observed event rate. However, many predicted probabilities are tied exactly at the quartile cut points.

(a) Explain why ties can prevent perfectly equal-sized quartile bins.
(b) Propose a principled strategy for handling ties to keep bin counts similar while avoiding arbitrary splitting that could distort interpretation.
(c) Explain how unequal bin sizes would affect the uncertainty (variance) of observed event rates across bins and how you would communicate this in a report/plot.
questions.correct_answer:
(a) Quartile cut points are based on order statistics. If many observations share the same predicted probability at a cut point, then placing all tied values on one side can push one bin above 25% and the adjacent bin below 25%, making exact equal counts impossible without splitting identical scores.

(b) A principled approach is to keep identical predicted probabilities together (do not split ties) and accept slight imbalance, or use a deterministic tie-handling rule that minimizes imbalance (e.g., choose cut points so that bins are as equal as possible subject to not splitting tied score values). Another acceptable approach is to jitter scores very slightly only for bin assignment (not for evaluation) with a fixed random seed, but this should be disclosed because it introduces an artificial ordering.

(c) Unequal bin sizes lead to different standard errors for observed event rates: larger bins have lower variance (narrower confidence intervals), smaller bins have higher variance (wider confidence intervals). This can make apparent gaps between predicted and observed rates more likely due to noise in small bins. In reporting, include bin counts and add error bars/confidence intervals for observed rates (or show a reliability diagram with uncertainty bands) so readers can judge whether deviations are meaningful.
questions.explanation: This answer addresses the core reason quartiles aim for equal counts (comparable precision) while acknowledging a realistic edge case (ties). It proposes tie-aware binning that preserves interpretability (identical scores treated consistently) and connects unequal bin sizes to differing uncertainty in observed rates, recommending transparent communication via counts and confidence intervals/error bars.

questions.question_text: A model outputs predicted probabilities for 1,000 cases, but the scores are highly concentrated near 0.05. You create a calibration plot using fixed-width bins: [0,0.2), [0.2,0.4), [0.4,0.6), [0.6,0.8), [0.8,1.0]. Which issue is most likely compared to using quartile (equal-frequency) bins?
options: Each bin will have identical counts, so the observed event rates will have identical standard errors
The observed event rates will be biased upward because fixed-width bins overestimate probabilities near 0
Some bins may contain very few observations, leading to much wider confidence intervals and unstable observed rates in those bins
Fixed-width bins cannot be used for calibration because they violate the definition of calibration
questions.explanation: With fixed-width bins, the number of observations per bin depends on the score distribution. If most scores fall near 0.05, the [0,0.2) bin will be large and stable, while higher-probability bins may be tiny or empty, making their observed rates extremely noisy (wide confidence intervals). Quartile binning avoids this by keeping counts similar across bins.

questions.question_text: In a calibration-by-quartiles plot, why is it important that each quartile bin contains (roughly) the same number of observations?
options: Because equal-sized bins ensure each bin covers the same probability width (e.g., 0.0–0.25, 0.25–0.50, etc.)
Because equal counts make the sampling variability of observed event rates more comparable across bins, making miscalibration comparisons fairer
Because equal counts guarantee the model is perfectly calibrated within each bin
Because equal counts eliminate the need to compute observed event rates (they will equal the predicted rates)
questions.explanation: Calibration compares mean predicted probability to the observed event rate per bin. If bins have very different sample sizes, the observed rates will have different variances (confidence intervals): large bins yield stable estimates while small bins are noisy. Equal-frequency (quartile) bins make these variances more comparable, so differences between predicted and observed are less confounded by unequal precision.

questions.question_text:
In Bayesian modeling of count-based processes, the gamma distribution is often used as a prior for the rate parameter.

Which statement best explains WHY a Gamma prior is commonly paired with a Poisson likelihood for an unknown rate parameter λ?

A. Because the Gamma distribution is defined only on integers, matching the Poisson support
B. Because the Gamma prior is conjugate to the Poisson likelihood, producing a posterior in the same family
C. Because the Gamma distribution is symmetric like the normal distribution, simplifying inference
D. Because the Poisson distribution models waiting times and the Gamma distribution models counts
options: A. Because the Gamma distribution is defined only on integers, matching the Poisson support
B. Because the Gamma prior is conjugate to the Poisson likelihood, producing a posterior in the same family
C. Because the Gamma distribution is symmetric like the normal distribution, simplifying inference
D. Because the Poisson distribution models waiting times and the Gamma distribution models counts
questions.explanation: A Gamma prior on λ is conjugate to a Poisson likelihood: after observing data, the posterior for λ is also Gamma, with updated parameters. This makes Bayesian updating analytically convenient. Option A is incorrect because Gamma is continuous on positive reals. Option C is incorrect because Gamma is generally skewed, not symmetric. Option D reverses typical roles: Poisson models counts; Gamma often models waiting times (or is used as a prior on rates).

questions.question_text:
A call center models a customer's total service time as the sum of two independent service phases, each with an exponential distribution and the same rate. 

1) What distribution is appropriate for the total service time? 
2) Give one practical reason (in words) why this model might be reasonable in queuing/service settings.
questions.correct_answer:
1) Gamma distribution (specifically an Erlang distribution with shape k = 2).
2) Many services occur in multiple sequential stages (e.g., verification then resolution). If each stage duration is approximately memoryless and independent (exponential), their sum becomes gamma/Erlang, capturing multi-phase service time variability better than a single exponential.
questions.explanation: The gamma distribution arises as the distribution of a sum of independent exponential random variables with a common rate; when the number of phases is an integer, it is often called an Erlang distribution. In service systems, tasks frequently consist of multiple sequential phases, so modeling total time as a sum of stage times is a natural application.

questions.question_text:
Which scenario is the MOST appropriate application of a gamma distribution?

A. Modeling the number of emails arriving in one minute
B. Modeling the time until the 5th customer arrives, assuming independent exponential inter-arrival times
C. Modeling a binary outcome such as whether a machine fails today (yes/no)
D. Modeling heights of adult humans
options: A. Modeling the number of emails arriving in one minute
B. Modeling the time until the 5th customer arrives, assuming independent exponential inter-arrival times
C. Modeling a binary outcome such as whether a machine fails today (yes/no)
D. Modeling heights of adult humans
questions.explanation: A gamma distribution commonly models waiting time until an event occurs multiple times; specifically, the sum of k independent exponential waiting times (e.g., time until the 5th arrival) follows a gamma distribution. Option A is typically modeled by a Poisson distribution (a count in a fixed interval). Option C is Bernoulli/Binomial. Option D is often approximated by a normal distribution.

questions.question_text:
You calibrate probabilities by group using:

```python
adjusted = np.clip(p + lam * (actual_rate - pred_rate), 0, 1)
```

Assume within a group you apply the same shift to every prediction (before clipping), i.e., p' = p + lam * delta with delta = actual_rate − pred_rate.

Ignoring clipping effects, what value of λ makes the *mean* of the adjusted probabilities equal the actual_rate for that group?

A) λ = 0
B) λ = 0.5
C) λ = 1
D) λ = pred_rate / actual_rate
options: λ = pred_rate / actual_rate
questions.explanation: Let mean(p) = pred_rate. After shifting, mean(p') = mean(p + λ*delta) = pred_rate + λ*(actual_rate − pred_rate). Setting mean(p') = actual_rate gives: pred_rate + λ*(actual_rate − pred_rate) = actual_rate ⇒ λ*(actual_rate − pred_rate) = (actual_rate − pred_rate) ⇒ λ = 1 (when actual_rate ≠ pred_rate). This shows λ=1 fully corrects the mean calibration error at the group level (before clipping).

questions.question_text:
A model produces predicted probabilities for a group with mean(predicted_prob) = 0.30. The observed event rate is mean(actual_outcome) = 0.42.

You want to apply scaled-delta calibration with λ = 0.5 to an individual prediction predicted_prob = 0.80.

1) Compute delta.
2) Compute the adjusted probability.
3) State whether clipping to [0,1] is needed in this case.
questions.correct_answer: 1) delta = 0.42 − 0.30 = 0.12. 2) adjusted_prob = 0.80 + 0.5 * 0.12 = 0.80 + 0.06 = 0.86. 3) Clipping is not needed because 0.86 is already within [0,1].
questions.explanation: Scaled-delta calibration uses adjusted_prob = predicted_prob + λ * delta with delta = actual_rate − predicted_rate. Here delta is positive (the model underpredicts on average), so probabilities increase. Clipping is only required if the update pushes values below 0 or above 1; 0.86 remains valid.

questions.question_text:
You are calibrating predicted probabilities using an additive delta. Let
- predicted_rate = mean(predicted_prob)
- actual_rate = mean(actual_outcome)
- delta = actual_rate − predicted_rate

Which formula correctly applies a scaling factor (lambda, λ) to control calibration strength?
options: adjusted_prob = predicted_prob + delta / λ
adjusted_prob = predicted_prob + λ * delta
adjusted_prob = λ * predicted_prob + delta
adjusted_prob = predicted_prob * (1 + λ) + delta
questions.explanation: The scaled-delta calibration modifies the original additive update by multiplying the delta by λ: adjusted_prob = predicted_prob + λ * (actual_rate − predicted_rate). This directly controls how much of the observed miscalibration (delta) you apply. λ=1 recovers the original method; λ=0 applies no change.

questions.question_text:
Consider a dataset with predictors (X1, X2, X3) used to predict a target Y. Suppose X3 is constructed as:

```text
X3 = 2*X1 - X2
```

Assume computations are exact (no noise) and an intercept is included in regressions.

1) What is the R² when you regress X3 on (X1, X2)?
2) What is the VIF for X3?
3) Explain why VIF becomes extreme here and what practical consequence this has for estimating the coefficient of X3 in a regression of Y on (X1, X2, X3).
questions.correct_answer: 1) R² = 1. 2) VIF = 1/(1 − 1) = ∞. 3) X3 is an exact linear combination of X1 and X2, so it is perfectly predictable from them in the auxiliary regression, giving R² = 1 and thus infinite VIF. In the main regression, this creates perfect multicollinearity: the design matrix is not full rank, coefficients are not uniquely identifiable (and standard errors/variance for the affected coefficients blow up or the model cannot be fit without dropping/regularizing a variable).
questions.explanation: Because X3 is exactly determined by X1 and X2, the auxiliary regression of X3 on (X1, X2) has zero residual variance, so R² = 1. Plugging into VIF = 1/(1 − R²) yields division by zero, i.e., infinite VIF. This corresponds to perfect multicollinearity: adding X3 provides no new linear information beyond X1 and X2, making coefficient estimation unstable or impossible (non-identifiability) in an ordinary least squares model without special handling.

questions.question_text:
A feature X_i is regressed on all other features and the auxiliary regression yields R² = 0.8.

1) Compute VIF for X_i.
2) By what factor is the standard error of the coefficient for X_i inflated relative to the case where X_i is uncorrelated with the other predictors?
questions.correct_answer: 1) VIF = 1/(1 − 0.8) = 1/0.2 = 5. 2) Standard error inflation factor = sqrt(VIF) = sqrt(5) ≈ 2.24.
questions.explanation: VIF is defined as 1/(1 − R²) using the R² from the regression of X_i on the other predictors. With R² = 0.8, VIF = 5. In linear regression theory, the variance of the estimated coefficient is inflated by VIF, so the standard error (square root of variance) is inflated by √VIF = √5 ≈ 2.24.

questions.question_text: In the context of Variance Inflation Factor (VIF), what does a large VIF for a feature X_i most directly indicate?
options: A. X_i has a strong causal effect on the target variable
B. X_i can be well predicted as a linear combination of the other features
C. X_i has low variance and should be standardized
D. X_i is uncorrelated with all other features
questions.explanation: VIF for X_i is computed by regressing X_i on the other predictors and using VIF = 1/(1 − R²). A large VIF means R² is close to 1, i.e., the other features explain most of the variance in X_i. That implies X_i is largely redundant (approximately a linear combination of the other features). VIF does not measure causality (A), does not diagnose low variance or the need for standardization (C), and uncorrelated predictors correspond to R² ≈ 0 and VIF ≈ 1 (D).

questions.question_text:
Let skewness be defined as Fisher’s moment coefficient:

$$\gamma_1 = \frac{E[(X-\mu)^3]}{\sigma^3}$$

Consider a feature used in an ML model that is **highly right-skewed** (large positive skewness). Answer the following:

1) Using the formula, explain *why* a few large right-tail values tend to make skewness positive.
2) Name **two** common preprocessing transformations that can reduce right-skewness, and briefly state why they help.
3) Give one reason highly skewed features can be problematic for models sensitive to outliers or distances.
questions.correct_answer:
1) In E[(X-μ)^3], observations above the mean have (X-μ) > 0, so their cubes are positive; large right-tail values make (X-μ)^3 very large and positive, dominating the expectation and making the numerator positive, hence γ1 > 0 (after scaling by σ^3).
2) Examples: (i) log transform (e.g., log(x) or log1p(x)) compresses large values more than small ones, shortening the right tail; (ii) Box–Cox (for positive data) or Yeo–Johnson (can handle nonpositive) chooses a power transform to make the distribution more symmetric; (iii) quantile transformation maps the feature to a target distribution (often normal), reducing skewness.
3) Skewness often implies extreme outliers and non-normality; outliers can disproportionately influence loss/parameter estimates in linear regression (e.g., least squares) and can dominate distance computations in k-NN/k-means, harming model fit or clustering structure.
questions.explanation: Skewness is the third standardized moment. Cubing deviations amplifies large-magnitude deviations and preserves sign: large values above the mean contribute large positive terms, while values below the mean contribute negative terms. In a right-skewed distribution, the right tail contains a few very large positive deviations that can dominate E[(X-μ)^3], yielding positive skewness. Transformations like log/Box–Cox/quantile reduce the influence of the right tail by compressing large values or remapping the distribution toward symmetry. Many ML methods are sensitive to outliers (least squares) or scale/distance (k-NN, k-means), so skewness can degrade performance unless addressed.

questions.question_text:
Two datasets are summarized as follows:

- **Dataset A:** Values are tightly clustered, but there are a few extreme outliers on the right.
- **Dataset B:** Values are spread widely but the distribution is symmetric.

Which pairing is most plausible?

(Assume CV refers to the coefficient of variation and skewness refers to Fisher's moment coefficient.)
options: A: high CV and skewness ≈ 0; B: low CV and positive skewness
A: low CV and positive skewness; B: high CV and skewness ≈ 0
A: low CV and negative skewness; B: high CV and positive skewness
A: high CV and negative skewness; B: low CV and skewness ≈ 0
questions.explanation: Dataset A is mostly concentrated (suggesting lower relative spread → lower CV) but has a long right tail due to a few large outliers (→ positive skewness). Dataset B is widely spread (→ higher CV) yet symmetric (→ skewness near 0). This matches option B.

questions.question_text: Which statement best interprets a distribution with **positive skewness**?
options: A symmetric distribution with equal tails on both sides
A left-skewed distribution with a longer tail to the left
A right-skewed distribution with a longer tail to the right
A distribution with high dispersion relative to its mean
questions.explanation: Positive skewness indicates asymmetry where the right tail is longer: most observations are concentrated on the left with a few large values pulling the tail to the right. Option D describes the coefficient of variation (relative dispersion), not skewness.

questions.question_text:
Consider the following implementation sketch:

```python
corr_matrix = X.corr(method="pearson").abs()
for col in corr_matrix.columns:
    other_corrs = corr_matrix.loc[col].drop(col, errors="ignore")
    max_corr = other_corrs.max()
    scores[col] = 1.0 - max_corr
```

(a) Explain what happens to `scores[col]` if `X` contains only a single feature column.

(b) Propose a robust modification (in words or code) to handle this case so that the redundancy score is well-defined.

(c) Briefly justify your chosen behavior (what value should the score be and why).
questions.correct_answer:
(a) With a single feature, `other_corrs` becomes an empty Series after dropping `col`, so `other_corrs.max()` yields `NaN` (no elements to take a max over). Therefore `scores[col] = 1.0 - NaN` becomes `NaN`.

(b) One robust fix is to detect the empty case (or fill missing max values) and set `max_corr = 0.0` when there are no other features:

```python
other_corrs = corr_matrix.loc[col].drop(col, errors="ignore")
max_corr = other_corrs.max() if len(other_corrs) > 0 else 0.0
scores[col] = 1.0 - max_corr
```

Alternatively:

```python
max_corr = other_corrs.max()
max_corr = 0.0 if pd.isna(max_corr) else max_corr
scores[col] = 1.0 - max_corr
```

(c) Setting `max_corr = 0.0` makes the score equal to 1.0, which matches the intended interpretation: if there are no other features, the feature cannot be redundant with anything else, so it should be maximally unique.
questions.explanation: The max-correlation step is undefined for an empty set of comparisons, which produces NaN in typical pandas behavior. A robust implementation must define what “maximum correlation with any other feature” means when there are no other features. Treating the maximum as 0 is consistent with the idea that redundancy is only assessed relative to other features; with none present, redundancy is zero and uniqueness (1 − 0) is one.

questions.question_text:
You have four features A, B, C, D with the following absolute correlation matrix (diagonal entries are 1):

|   | A   | B   | C   | D   |
|---|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| A | 1.0 | 0.80| 0.10| 0.40|
| B | 0.80| 1.0 | 0.50| 0.20|
| C | 0.10| 0.50| 1.0 | 0.60|
| D | 0.40| 0.20| 0.60| 1.0 |

Using the described method, which feature has the highest redundancy score (i.e., is most unique)?
options: A. A
B. B
C. C
D. D
questions.explanation:
Compute max absolute correlation with any other feature and then 1 − max:
- A: max(0.80, 0.10, 0.40) = 0.80 → score = 0.20
- B: max(0.80, 0.50, 0.20) = 0.80 → score = 0.20
- C: max(0.10, 0.50, 0.60) = 0.60 → score = 0.40
- D: max(0.40, 0.20, 0.60) = 0.60 → score = 0.40
C and D tie at 0.40, but the question asks which feature has the highest score; among the provided single-choice options, D is a correct selection (C would also be correct if multi-select were allowed).

questions.question_text: In the redundancy score computation described, which statement best describes what the score measures for a feature?
options: A. The average absolute Pearson correlation of the feature with all other features
B. One minus the maximum absolute Pearson correlation of the feature with any other feature
C. The maximum (signed) Pearson correlation of the feature with any other feature
D. The variance of the feature after standardizing all features
questions.explanation: The procedure takes the absolute Pearson correlation matrix, then for each feature finds the maximum correlation with any other feature (excluding itself), and converts it to a uniqueness score via 1 − max_corr. This makes highly correlated (redundant) features score near 0 and more unique features score nearer 1.

questions.question_text:
A colleague runs a Shapiro–Wilk test and gets p = 0.03, concluding “the data are non-normal, so we’re done.” You suspect the deviation from normality might be due to tail behavior rather than a shift in the center.

Explain (1) why a Q–Q plot can be more informative than a single p-value for assessing normality, and (2) what specific visual features you would look for in the Q–Q plot to diagnose *how* the data depart from normality (e.g., tail heaviness or skew).
questions.correct_answer:
A Q–Q plot is more informative because it shows the *pattern* of departure from the target distribution, not just a binary reject/do-not-reject decision. A p-value (e.g., from Shapiro–Wilk) summarizes evidence against normality and is sensitive to sample size, but it does not indicate whether the issue is skewness, heavy tails, light tails, or outliers.

In the Q–Q plot, I would look for: (a) overall linearity (approximate normality), (b) systematic curvature: an upward bend on the right end suggests a heavier right tail (more extreme high values than normal), a downward bend on the left end suggests a heavier left tail (more extreme low values than normal), and an S-shaped pattern indicates skewness (one tail heavier than the other). Points far from the line at the extremes suggest outliers or tail problems even if the center aligns well.
questions.explanation: This answer addresses both parts: it contrasts what a hypothesis test provides (a single significance result) with what a Q–Q plot provides (a diagnostic shape). It then links specific Q–Q plot deviations (end behavior and curvature/S-shape) to interpretable distributional features like tail heaviness and skewness, which is the key advantage emphasized in the source content.

questions.question_text: You create a normal Q–Q plot and observe that the points are close to the line in the middle, but the rightmost points (largest observations) curve upward above the diagonal line. What is the most appropriate interpretation?
options: The data have a heavier right tail than a normal distribution (more extreme high values than expected)
The data have a heavier left tail than a normal distribution (more extreme low values than expected)
The data are uniformly distributed
The data are approximately normal because the center aligns well
questions.explanation: In a normal Q–Q plot, the x-axis shows theoretical normal quantiles and the y-axis shows the sample quantiles. If the right end bends upward (sample quantiles exceed theoretical quantiles), the largest observed values are larger than what a normal distribution would predict—indicating a heavier right tail (positive skew / high-end outliers).

questions.question_text: In a normal Q–Q plot used to assess whether a dataset is approximately normally distributed, what pattern most strongly suggests the data are close to normal?
options: The points lie roughly along a straight diagonal line
The points form a U-shaped curve with both ends above the line
The points cluster into two separate diagonal bands
The points form a horizontal line across the middle of the plot
questions.explanation: A Q–Q plot compares sample quantiles (your sorted data) to theoretical quantiles (expected values from a target distribution, often normal). If the data follow the target distribution, corresponding quantiles match and the points fall approximately on a straight diagonal line. Systematic curvature or other structure indicates departures from the target distribution.

questions.question_text: A forecaster outputs probabilities for a binary event and you evaluate them with a scoring rule. Which choice of scoring rule is **most appropriate** if your target is **continuous** and you have the model's full predictive CDF \(F\)?
options: Logarithmic score (log loss), because it is strictly proper for any outcome type as long as you have probabilities.
Brier score, because it is the standard proper scoring rule for any target type and decomposes into reliability/resolution/uncertainty.
